key_insight: The paper introduces a damage-and-fracture constitutive model for additively manufactured polymer TPMS lattices that combines viscoplastic deformation with a new damage initiation criterion to predict failure under complex loading.
engineering_relevance: This enables more reliable in silico design of lightweight architected polymer structures by linking lattice geometry, loading history, and fracture behavior.
method: Implemented a high-fidelity rate-dependent viscoplastic model in Abaqus/Explicit via a user material subroutine.
Added a damage initiation criterion based on stored elastic energy and equivalent plastic strain for amorphous polymers.
Used a gradient-damage framework to simulate fracture and validate against experiments.
evidence_signal: The model was validated against systematic experiments on unit cells and lattices under tension, compression, and non-monotonic loading, with quantitative and qualitative agreement reported for a unit cell and tensile lattice tests.
watch_item: The approach depends on calibration and successful implementation of coupled viscoplastic-plus-gradient-damage models, which may limit transferability to other materials, geometries, or loading rates without re-tuning.
paper_title: Modeling damage and fracture in additively manufactured polymeric triply periodic minimal surface lattices
